edition.editionTitle: The Poetic Edda: Translated from the Icelandic with an Introduction and Notes
edition.licenseName: Public domain in the United States
edition.attributionText: Hávamál, translated by Henry Adams Bellows, in The Poetic Edda (1923). Public-domain transcription sourced from Project Gutenberg ebook 73533.
edition.publicDomainJurisdictionNotes: Recorded for United States publication status. Recheck rules for other jurisdictions before commercial reuse.
edition.sourceNotes: Bundled passages were transcribed against the Project Gutenberg HTML edition and marked published only after line-level review. Complete-corpus ingestion remains an editorial import task.

text_lines: Within the gates | ere a man shall go,
(Full warily let him watch,)
Full long let him look about him;
For little he knows | where a foe may lurk,
And sit in the seats within.
passages.alignment_note: Bellows numbering is the internal comparison anchor only; it is not presented as the academically definitive division.

text_lines: Hail to the giver! | a guest has come;
Where shall the stranger sit?
Swift shall he be | who with swords shall try
The proof of his might to make.
passages.alignment_note: Bellows numbering is the internal comparison anchor only; it is not presented as the academically definitive division.

text_lines: Fire he needs | who with frozen knees
Has come from the cold without;
Food and clothes | must the farer have,
The man from the mountains come.
passages.alignment_note: Bellows numbering is the internal comparison anchor only; it is not presented as the academically definitive division.

text_lines: Water and towels | and welcoming speech
Should he find who comes to the feast;
If renown he would get, | and again be greeted,
Wisely and well must he act.
passages.alignment_note: Bellows numbering is the internal comparison anchor only; it is not presented as the academically definitive division.

text_lines: Wits must he have | who wanders wide,
But all is easy at home;
At the witless man | the wise shall wink
When among such men he sits.
passages.alignment_note: Bellows numbering is the internal comparison anchor only; it is not presented as the academically definitive division.

text_lines: A man shall not boast | of his keenness of mind,
But keep it close in his breast;
To the silent and wise | does ill come seldom
When he goes as guest to a house;
(For a faster friend | one never finds
Than wisdom tried and true.)
passages.alignment_note: Bellows numbering is the internal comparison anchor only; it is not presented as the academically definitive division.

text_lines: The knowing guest | who goes to the feast,
In silent attention sits;
With his ears he hears, | with his eyes he watches,
Thus wary are wise men all.
passages.alignment_note: Bellows numbering is the internal comparison anchor only; it is not presented as the academically definitive division.

text_lines: Happy the one | who wins for himself
Favor and praises fair;
Less safe by far | is the wisdom found
That is hid in another’s heart.
passages.alignment_note: Bellows numbering is the internal comparison anchor only; it is not presented as the academically definitive division.

text_lines: Happy the man | who has while he lives
Wisdom and praise as well,
For evil counsel | a man full oft
Has from another’s heart.
passages.alignment_note: Bellows numbering is the internal comparison anchor only; it is not presented as the academically definitive division.

text_lines: A better burden | may no man bear
For wanderings wide than wisdom;
It is better than wealth | on unknown ways,
And in grief a refuge it gives.
passages.alignment_note: Bellows numbering is the internal comparison anchor only; it is not presented as the academically definitive division.

text_lines: A better burden | may no man bear
For wanderings wide than wisdom;
Worse food for the journey | he brings not afield
Than an over-drinking of ale.
passages.alignment_note: Bellows numbering is the internal comparison anchor only; it is not presented as the academically definitive division.

text_lines: Less good there lies | than most believe
In ale for mortal men;
For the more he drinks | the less does man
Of his mind the mastery hold.
passages.alignment_note: Bellows numbering is the internal comparison anchor only; it is not presented as the academically definitive division.

text_lines: Fire for men | is the fairest gift,
And power to see the sun;
Health as well, | if a man may have it,
And a life not stained with sin.
passages.alignment_note: Bellows numbering is the internal comparison anchor only; it is not presented as the academically definitive division.

text_lines: All wretched is no man, | though never so sick;
Some from their sons have joy,
Some win it from kinsmen, | and some from their wealth,
And some from worthy works.
passages.alignment_note: Bellows numbering is the internal comparison anchor only; it is not presented as the academically definitive division.

text_lines: It is better to live | than to lie a corpse,
The live man catches the cow;
I saw flames rise | for the rich man’s pyre,
And before his door he lay dead.
passages.alignment_note: Bellows numbering is the internal comparison anchor only; it is not presented as the academically definitive division.

text_lines: The lame rides a horse, | the handless is herdsman,
The deaf in battle is bold;
The blind man is better | than one that is burned,
No good can come of a corpse.
passages.alignment_note: Bellows numbering is the internal comparison anchor only; it is not presented as the academically definitive division.

text_lines: A son is better, | though late he be born,
And his father to death have fared;
Memory-stones | seldom stand by the road
Save when kinsman honors his kin.
passages.alignment_note: Bellows numbering is the internal comparison anchor only; it is not presented as the academically definitive division.

text_lines: Two make a battle, | the tongue slays the head;
In each furry coat | a fist I look for.
passages.alignment_note: Bellows numbering is the internal comparison anchor only; it is not presented as the academically definitive division.

text_lines: He welcomes the night | whose fare is enough.
(Short are the yards of a ship,)
Uneasy are autumn nights;
Full oft does the weather | change in a week,
And more in a month’s time.
passages.alignment_note: Bellows numbering is the internal comparison anchor only; it is not presented as the academically definitive division.

text_lines: A man knows not, | if nothing he knows,
That gold oft apes begets;
One man is wealthy | and one is poor,
Yet scorn for him none should know.
passages.alignment_note: Bellows numbering is the internal comparison anchor only; it is not presented as the academically definitive division.

text_lines: Among Fitjung’s sons | saw I well-stocked folds,—
Now bear they the beggar’s staff;
Wealth is as swift | as a winking eye,
Of friends the falsest it is.
passages.alignment_note: Bellows numbering is the internal comparison anchor only; it is not presented as the academically definitive division.

text_lines: Cattle die, | and kinsmen die,
And so one dies one’s self;
But a noble name | will never die,
If good renown one gets.
passages.alignment_note: Bellows numbering is the internal comparison anchor only; it is not presented as the academically definitive division.

text_lines: Cattle die, | and kinsmen die,
And so one dies one’s self;
One thing I know | that never dies,
The fame of a dead man’s deeds.
passages.alignment_note: Bellows numbering is the internal comparison anchor only; it is not presented as the academically definitive division.

text_lines: Certain is that | which is sought from runes,
That the gods so great have made,
And the Master-Poet painted;
..... of the race of gods:
Silence is safest and best.
passages.alignment_note: Bellows numbering is the internal comparison anchor only; it is not presented as the academically definitive division.
